No `windows` for `self._manipulate_and_refresh(new=window=windows)`.

This is for scrolling only...

When we start scrolling, we get the real data first time.... then we start faking things... and everytime we scroll (still on the bar), we continue to work with fake data in the loop (because of no new events... so no new data)

If events changes, we would just work with that data... then fake again. This function does not know which one is real or not... since we just shift things... then push out via the same `self._manipulate_and_refresh(new_window=windows)`.

Short version... Things are fake as shit when we scroll.

It's a little more subtle.  The logic should be:
1) If the number of bundles increases then the focus should be on the new bundles.
2) If the number of bundles decreases, then focus should be on the same bundle as before unless that bundle doesn't exist anymore, in which case we select the closest bundle that does exist, where closest means 'next' by default or 'last' if there is no next bundle.

Yup: the request has been authenticated, but it doesn't have the right permissions.

The ASAP spec lists RFC 7235 as a reference, but never actually references it. Section 2.1 of RFC 7235 states:

> A server that receives valid credentials that are not adequate to
> gain access ought to respond with the 403 (Forbidden) status code
> (Section 6.5.3 of [RFC7231]).

Section 6.5.3 of RFC 7231 covers 403 responses, about which it says:

> If authentication credentials were provided in the request, the server considers them
> insufficient to grant access.

No, I meant to use `metrics.block_h`/`w`. `metrics.block_h` contains the height of a block, where `block_h` contains either that or the height of a block in the bottom row if the image height is shorter than an exact multiple of `metrics.block_h`, so `y * metrics.block_h` is the offset used to move `j` into the current block.

I think we're going to have to move beyond `grep` to fix this.  The following uses `awk` (with emulation of `grep`'s colour output) to do the check with a little more sophistication, but should get the behaviour we're after and lets us include the `cpp`/`h` files in `tests`:

```bash
found="$($(awk 'function prefix(filename, lineno) {
    return "    \033[35m" filename "\033[36m:\033[32m" lineno "\033[36m:\033[0m"
}
function mark(pattern, string) { sub(pattern, "\033[01;31m&\033[0m", string); return string }
last && /^\s*{/ {
    print prefix(FILENAME, FNR-1) mark("\\)\\s*$", last)
    print prefix(FILENAME, FNR)   mark("^\\s*{", $0)
    last=""
}
{ last = /(if|for|while|catch|switch)\s*\(.*\)\s*$/ ? $0 : "" }
' `find include -type f` tests/*.{cpp,h} docs/*.rst)"
if [ -n "$found" ]; then
    check_style_errors=1
    echo -e '\033[31;01mError: braces should occur on the same line as the if/while/.. statement. Found issues in the following files:\033[0m'
    echo "$found"
fi
```

Switching to `py::object` would be the best case. There would be no restrictions on `py::arg_t` then. As a bonus, the template code would also be simplified since `py::arg_t` would no longer be a template class (it would only have a template constructor). `py::arg_t` should create it's `py::object` using the `automatic` policy which looks like the only reasonable choice. 

There is just one small inconsistency that this would introduce: Keyword arguments would then be passed to function calls with the `automatic` policy, while positional arguments would use `automatic_reference` (which is the function call default). I don't think this a big issue, but maybe you have some thoughts.

Hmm, I think this is too strong. We didn't deprecate the ``py::metaclass(handle)`` constructors, which permits creating pybind11-bound classes with custom metaclasses (fairly powerful IMO).

How about the following instead? The ``py::metaclass`` attribute is no longer required for binding classes with static properties. It is still needed in rare cases, e.g.  to set a custom metaclasses for pybind11-bound data structures.

Please don't store the defaults in a global. If there are identically named parameters they will conflict.

Also, you don't need to store the defaults separately at all since you can access the default values of the defined options of a command (in this case `run`) like this:
```
optionname_to_default = {param.name: param.get_default(ctx) for param in run.params}
```

Since we've modified the command signature, the command syntax will show in help as `[p]reason <reason>`.

You can actually specify a custom signature with the [usage](https://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/rewrite/ext/commands/api.html#discord.ext.commands.Command.usage) argument to `@commands.command()`. I think this is the best way to make it clear how the command can be used :)
